title: Cuisinart DGB-800 Fully Automatic Burr Grind & Brew, 12-Cup Glass, Silver
description:
About this item
Please refer to user guide or user manual or user guide (provided below in PDF) before first use
reviews_5s:
I love this coffee maker. It makes coffee precisely and just the way I like it every time, regardless of the number of cups selected. The front control panel is a little challenging to figure out when it comes to programming, but once you get that set, you're good to go. It is easy to clean and works like a charm.

But... Mine failed after about 10 months - it stopped sensing whether there was water in the reservoir and would not brew. However, I called customer service, explained the problem, sent a photo and had a replacement within 2 days. Surprisingly outstanding customer service and warranty support.

Now, I had given this very same model to a friends two years ago, and theirs is still working perfectly and they love it. So while I don't know exactly why mine failed, I don't think it is necessarily a problem with the model itself. And the warranty support was excellent. Highly recommended.There are some negative reviews for this DGB-800 but all I can do is review it in comparison to the Krups. It is easier to setup, grinds faster and more quietly, brews in a similar time, and makes more and stronger coffee.

You need to use decent quality filters of the correct size. If you happen to have some dirt cheap ones you got at the dollar store lying around, well those probably aren't the 10-12 cup size this requires. If we use cheap filters it overflows slightly. If we use Bunn filters it works fine with no overflowing issues. I guess the Bunn filters hold their shape more when wet, allowing coffee to drain down faster.

Are there things I'd like to see different? The water filling flap is narrow, not too narrow, but they could have come up with a better geometry to make it easier to fill without spilling. The coffee reservoir is very wide and shallow, I'd prefer it taller and not covering most of the top of the machine. A regular shape would be nice too, then I could make a two pound extension like I did for the Krups. I don't like having to refill the bean reservoir every few times I make coffee, the beans are no worse off in the machine than in the bag.

Definitely an upgrade.The DGB-800 is our fourth grind and brew type coffee maker. The last one was a Cuisinart DGB-475 that lasted so long I can't remember when we bought it. Maybe 13 years ago? It started having problems with the pump and the white plastic was getting pretty nasty looking. We bought a GEVI coffee maker before this new one, and it quit working after a few uses. It has a defective design where a hatch cover gets steamed up and jams, there is really no way to fix it. Couldn't get support or contact the seller and ended up dumping it. Total junk and sleazy seller.

So back to the DGB-800. This is finally a model we can get behind. It's about as good as our first grind and brew style maker, a Melitta that was designed very well. Unlike the DGB-475, the DGB-800 does not steam up the grinding area. Well, maybe it does but you can't see it, and there's no moisture collecting in the bean hopper. We like our coffee on the strong side, and the fine burr grind along with the mesh filter makes really great coffee. Quite a contrast from the blade grinder and paper filters.

Only one complaint, there's no way to turn on the hotplate once it shuts off? That's a silly oversight. I'm tempted to add a switch. The alternative is to set the timeout to 4 hours and turn it off when you're done.

So far, highly recommend this coffee maker. We'll be pleased if it lasts anywhere near as long as the last one, and I'll update the review if any issues arise.This is a really great coffee pot. I prefer whole bean coffee, fresh ground when I make a pot, so this cuts out a few steps for me each morning. I’ve used it almost everyday for a year now, and still working great like it’s brand new. The grind function is a little loud, but that’s to be expected. It took a couple tries to figure out the strength setting I preferred, but I very quickly figured out what makes the perfect cup for me.I bought this machine 2 weeks ago and I am super pleased with it so far. I feel like I should be paid by someone to review grind and brew coffee machines because I have owned so many of them over the past 3 or 4 years. And I have gotten to know them well through trial, error, and breakage. Of course I am not being paid for this review, but I am giving one because I think I can help someone else who'e in the same boat. Earlier this year I purchased and reviewed a Krups grind and brew (the newest model) which I liked very much but it died after about 8 months. It did not do well with oily beans and I constantly had to clean it. It also was not great at regulating the strength of the coffee, which has been pretty consistently a factor on all the machines I've owned, including several Cuisinarts. Other machines I've had and reviewed in this category besides past Cuisinarts include Breville, Capresso, Kenmore, and Gourmia. This Cuisinart comes with an auto clean feature and is one reason I was willing to spend more money for it. It has a large air tight hopper which is very convenient. I love that it's 12 cups and not 10, which almost all others are, because I do have company regularly and want to be able to make large quantities. I also greatly appreciate that you can set the hot plate to 4 hours so if I make a large pot I can sip it all morning at the proper temperature without microwaving. I don't know why anyone would buy the thermal carafe over the glass one with hot plate because I have yet to use a thermal carafe that keeps coffee hot even a half hour after brewing. This machine really seems to adjust the grinder properly to set the strength of coffee. It is easy to use. It has the newest technology to optimize the grounds. It is a bit slow, but reasonable for good coffee. The loudness is no different than other machines. Everything fits well together and operates properly. The carafe is easy to pour from. Previously I was not impressed with the Cuisinart design of their older grind and brews, but I am so grateful to have found this machine and highly recommend it!

UPDATE 12/15/18: After 2 months I am still impressed and in love with this machine. It never clogs - even with oily beans. This is a first from all those other grind and brews I have owned. It offers a variety of ways to control strength and taste, which I love. It has a large pot that can keep hot for up to 4 hours, which I also love - and it was easy to program (electronics are not my strong suit). It is relatively quiet and brews at a normal speed. It is large but fits under my cabinets on a moveable tray without issue. No complaints at all on this machine, and I am a critical type. I really don't understand the critical reviews at all on this product and seems like user error issues. I have no problems with it working properly, no overflows, and flavor of coffee is awesome. Who cares that you can't program it with ground coffee? It's a GRIND AND BREW machine!This is my 3rd grind and brew in 9 years. I absolutely love this coffeemaker. The new model has several changes. The machine is very sleek and nice looking, but bigger and takes up a bit more counter space. Nothing obscene. The bean hopper is wider but not deep enough, so it holds less coffee beans and you have to refill more often. The previous held up to half lb. of beans. The carafe has a wider opening making it seem bigger but it is a 12 cup. The water reservoir is bigger and you can easily fill it up from the right or left side. The coffee basket comes as one piece now with the lid that lifts to remove the filter for cleaning. Very easy peasy. I can’t believe people complained about having to clean the 3 pieces after every use on the previous model. It doesn’t take long. The coffee chute no longer comes apart on this model and no more scraping coffee buildup. There’s a setting for cleaning the chute and grinder. There’s one thing I am completely unhappy about this new model and I read up about - amount of ground coffee. I have adjusted the settings many times to conserve coffee and selected regular strength, but no luck. This model grinds a lot of beans per cup, so it seems you use more coffee weekly. I will have to live with it because I love my grind and brew, and the smell of fresh brewed coffee every morning in my home that resembles a coffeehouse. Just delightful. One last thing - the reason I’ve owned 3 in about 9 years. The first one had its heating plate/element burn out after the 3 years warranty. The second one I just replaced lasted a bit over 5 years but the exact same thing happened - heating plate/element burned out. It must be a Cuisinart defect on these models. Whatever it is, it needs to be fixed. I will keep enjoying every cup of coffee in this new model, and take in the aroma of fresh ground beans every morning.We love this grind & brew coffee maker. I recommend it and would buy it again! Coffee tastes great with freshly ground beans.I was able to purchase this coffee maker on Amazon for about $50 less than at a retail store. The package with the coffee maker was well packed (box in a box) and the carafe arrived in one piece. Cuisinart could not make it any easier to burr grind beans and have fresh ground coffee. Turn the coffee maker “on.” The coffee bean hopper holds 1/2 pound of beans and just pour them in. Pour water into the reservoir. Next, set the grind strength and the number of cups of coffee. Press brew. Within 5 - 7 minutes, you will have a great cup of coffee. I used Hawaiian beans, and wow the coffee was great. Ok, I skipped the thermal carafe as the warmer will continue to warm the coffee for up to 2 hrs and I did not want to drop additional bling for something that makes little difference to me. I did try the coffee after 2 hrs too and it still tasted great not burnt and still hot. I opted out of purchasing the "blade" grinder Cuisinart coffee maker though less money, as the blade grinder chops the beans unevenly resulting in entire beans, 3/4 beans and ground beans. The Burr grinder is worth the extra money to get consistency in the grind and of course the best tasting coffee.This has been quite a journey, this is my third coffee maker and I couldn’t be happier with the result! But let me just tell you how I got here:
First of all, I’m a Colombian woman who absolutely LOVES a good cup of coffee (I bring my own gourmet coffee from Colombia) so one morning I woke up as usual, got ready for work, went downstairs to make my coffee just to find out my coffee maker was broken!!!!! Don’t panic I had a French press ! Lol so anyways, I started my journey trying to find a good machine that had a built in grinder, so I order a cuisinart DGB550, tried it for a couple of times but I was extremely disappointed: the grinder would only hold 2 cups at a time, on top of that you have to take it a part every time to clean it before making a new cup, but the problem is that all the steam gets in to the grinder and make your beans wet and soggy making it impossible to use it multiple times.
I requested a refund and sent it back. Then I found the Cuisinart DGB 800, I ordered it and received a refurbished machine!!! I could not believe it when I opened the box and the machine was dirty, scratched and clearly used before, so guess what? Yes! I had to send it back again and wait 3 more days! I finally got my new baby and I couldn’t be happier! This one holds up to half a pound of coffee beans, it self cleans, the steam never reaches the compartment and you have the option to choose the strength of the coffee and the amount of cups, the reservoir holds 12 cups. The coffee is always hot and fresh and the smell of fresh grounded coffee is just amazing!
All I can say is that all the hustle was totally worth it! I super recommend this machine, just make sure you get what you paid for!I have had several Cuisinart grind and brew machines over the years and a Melita grind and brew too. Usually, the heating elements go and it is time for a new one. Yes I descale them regularly as I have hard water. This one is now about a year and half old and runs like a charm. The self cleaning grind mechanism is the best thing about it. No more cleaning out the gunk from the chute where the grinds come out. As far as the number of cups, remember a cup of coffee is not 8oz. but usually is 6oz. so when you fill up your morning mug it probably will take more than a cup on the carafe. I think they finally have got it right with this model.
reviews_1s:
UPDATE - This is a poorly made piece of junk. Since the review written below, coffee maker #2 broke with the same problem coffee maker #1 arrived with. I did a warranty exchange and got coffee maker #3, which eventually started grinding the same amount of beans regardless of setting and now will not brew at all. Three coffee makers, all broken despite being maintained properly. Not even 1 year. Next time I'm just buying a basic drip coffee maker and decent grinder.

I'd normally have given this 4 stars but I ran into some quality control issues that isn't really acceptable for a fairly expensive coffee maker. I bought this to replace an existing all in one burr grinder coffee maker that had started giving up the ghost.

When I first attempted brew a pot, the coffee maker didn't do anything other than hum and a couple drops of water dripped out. I thought maybe the burr grinder was jammed so I emptied the hopper and tried my best to get all the beans out of the grinder. Here's the first design problem - there's no way to really access the grinder in any meaningful fashion. If it's jammed, you're SOL, you can't get anything in there to clear it. Maybe it was jammed, maybe it wasn't. I have no idea.

I kept trying to get it to work, and the coffee maker refused to even attempt to grind. It would keep alerting me that I hadn't cleared the basket from the previous attempt... but it was empty and I had opened and closed it multiple times. Attempts to just run a basic no-grind drip and maybe force a reset were met with the same sort of hum as if it was still trying to grind. I gave up, returned it, and got a replacement. This really annoyed me especially considering my previous coffee maker also required replacement (twice in fact) when I first got it.

Coffee maker number 2, I ran a basic no-grind, no-coffee cycle to just flush out the system as suggested in the manual. That ran fine, and then I attempted to make a normal grind. Because I hadn't opened the basket between the flush and my attempt to make coffee, it refused to grind with it thinking the basket had not been emptied. Again, as suggested by the directions, I stopped the cycle opened the basket, reseated it, closed it and tried again. It still refused to grind. It was stuck in a state of thinking the basket was full. I tried many combinations reseating the basket, but nothing worked. I finally got it to work after resorting to unplugging and plugging it back in. Which brings up design issue #2 - there's no way to override what it thinks its state is. You can't just say "do it anyway". That's a relatively minor issue provided that a power cycle always resets it.

So now it's been working fine. How does it work in normal operation? It's acceptable.

Pros: 12 cup capacity is nice, and the hopper is decently sized. Grinder is also relatively quiet for an inexpensive burr grinder.

Con: This is a big one for me, and what knocks a star off - you can't adjust the fineness of the grind. The coffee I like tastes best when it's more finely ground than the grinder is set to. That means I have to set the grind amount higher than the number of cups I'm making. So if I'm making 10 cups, I have to set the grind to 12 cups. I feel like I'm just wasting coffee when I could use less coffee if I could set a finer grind.

This would be a really great coffee maker if you could just set the grind fineness.I've been meaning to write a review on this purchase and I'm finally getting around to it. I'll start by saying I was in the market for a fully automatic coffee maker. And, by fully automatic, I wanted a coffee maker that I could program to grind and brew by simply setting a program time. This pot by all intensive purposes fit that description. Upon receiving the coffee maker it appears to be in good shape, packaging was in tact and there was no obvious damage to the unit. After thoroughly cleaning all the parts, I set up my new coffee pot for a fresh pot of coffee the next morning. It was set to start grinding beans and begin brewing around 6:30 in the morning. Now, I'm not sure what time it actually started brewing which takes me to my first and biggest issue with this coffee maker. I have to reset the clock on this piece of junk every night. I've actually woken up to a cold pot of coffee because the pot brewed somewhere thought the night?!? The clock does whatever it wants...no rhyme or reason. Sometimes it runs slow, sometimes it speeds off at a cheetah like pace disregarding any and all rules of timekeeping. I swear I've see sundials keep better time than this piece of garbage. In fact, if there was a way for me to swap the current digital clock for a sundial I'm quite certain I'd have better luck having hot coffee in the morning. Now, lets deal with the noise it makes when it grinds beans. While I understand the process of grinding beans is going to make a little noise, the very first morning this thing took off (and I'm certain it wasn't at the time I set it to start because, well, it can't keep time) I can assure you I didn't need coffee to wake me up at that point. It's so loud I thought a CH-47 Chinook helicopter was landing in my living room! When that sucker spooled up, I came up out of my bed with my fightin' mitts on! And when it finished grinding a solid 20 seconds later, then and only then did it actually start to function as a semi-normal coffee pot! I'm pretty sure that my neighbors use my coffee pot as their alarm clock it's so loud. So, I'll summarize by saying it's a coffee pot and it brews coffee. But, I grind the beans the night before when EVERYONE is still awake. Then, every morning I walk to the kitchen and manually turn on the coffee pot to make sure I have hot coffee...not coffee that I have to reheat in a microwave because my pot randomly brewed coffee at 3am because the piece of junk can keep time to save it's life. It's not just a story its my life and morning routine. Thanks Cuisinart, you almost nailed it.**Update*. . Never again will I buy any Conair/Cuisinart products due to customer service. Waited 10 days, no response from sending cut cord picture, spending a lot of money on coffee pods to "get by" waiting for replacement machine. I finally gave up, called and told them to cancel sending a replacement machine, it's been a month and am done waiting and dealing with it. First machine I received didnt work, returned it and bought another one. Second machine Broke after 5 months. Called company 8/31. To get replacement you have to cut the cord off "close to the back of the machine, then lay the cord next to the machine take a picture" to prove you can no longer use the machine, and send it to an email address . I told them I would have no way to make coffee then, they said I could wait and send picture after I received replacement, but new machine would not be warranted until they got picture. Called back 9/9, was told it takes 5-7 business days, before it ships, I hadn't waited long enough. Called 9/16 - was told they were waiting on the picture of the cut cord. Will not buy again. Almost 200 dollars and then you have to buy another coffee maker to get by until they ship you a new one? the coffee is great while the machine works ...I bought a Keurig to get by until replacement arrived, have spent 100 dollars on pods. Now buying a regular coffee maker, and a grinder. Whole experience has cost me over 400 dollars only to be seriously disappointed... valuable lessonUPDATED!
the longer I have this the worse it gets! Coffee grounds are all over the machine. I have no idea how this is happening. I guess I was spoiled by my first grind and brew maker but if this is the best it can do, you can KEEP IT! I'm not that hard to please but a mouth full of grounds makes me want to PUKE! That's so gross! I can't take anymore of it. I added 3 pictures, the 3rd is the bottom of the pot with what didn't get into my cup. It may not seem like a lot in the picture but there was a ton of grounds in my cup. Look at the other pics, the coffee is all over the place. I'm not sure how it get's so much all over everything like that. When I tried to use the screen that came with the maker, it was like drinking pond scum, I don't use those screen type of filters for that reason. If I have to drink scum, I'll quit drinking coffee! I only hope the next one is better, I see some bad reviews but I read bad reviews on everything so far and how do you get through the bad and find one you're going to really enjoy? So far, my new machine is better, I just need to get it figured out how to get it set properly. Link to a better machine below!
http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002EVOVPI/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_asin_title_o00_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1
After having an awesome grind and brew coffee maker for about 10 years, I had to retire it. Sadly the brand was Kenmore and it's no longer available.
I looked around and found some reviews on the most popular brands. The first one had the thermal carafe and no hot plate. The coffee was ok but somewhat cool. The 2nd cup was cold! I guess I had to make 12 cups to make it warm.
So this is my 2nd grind and brew coffee maker in a week! I'm not that thrilled with this one either. The coffee is a little weak and I have grounds in my coffee just like the other one. The whole basket is just a mess when the brewing is complete. I'm not sure how it dies this except it must load the water too quickly. The worst part is if you don't leave the filter basket in place, it will shut off. It's supposed to be a programmable shut off, but remove that basket, the warming plate shuts off and your next cup is lukewarm at best. I only figured out that I have to leave the basket in, by mistake. So I tried to make my coffee stronger by setting it to grind 8 cups and only add the 6 cups of water. All it did was add more grounds to my coffee and add to the mess. I guess I'll keep this until I find something better. The cost of this maker is very high for the quality of coffee it delivers.
I love the coffee with the fresh ground beans! That is the best coffee I've had! All the best coffee in stores are made with fresh ground beans. That is how I like my coffee made now and hope I can find something better in the near future.I purchased this coffee maker in December 2020 and it has had problems since the day it arrived up until now, and I’ve officially had enough. For the first 2 months after receiving the machine, we would wake up to coffee that had leaked out of the basket and onto the counter, down into the drawers and cabinets, and onto the floor. This seemed to be due to the basket not being large enough to accommodate the volume of grinds needed to make a 12 cup pot of coffee and, as a result, the water not flowing through the basket quickly enough. To ensure this didn’t happen, we had to make smaller pots of coffee and, as an added preventative measure, put adhesive tape over a seemingly pointless notch on the side of the coffee basket that allowed coffee to leak out if the water level in the basket got too high. Things were ok for about a week, after which we would wake up to extremely weak coffee or a pot full of hot water, even though the machine had been set to the highest coffee strength. Now it seemed that the coffee beans and grinds were building up in the grinder portion of the machine, resulting in the machine grinding few to no beans. This seemed like it would be a simple fix; just clean out the burr grinder, right? Wrong. The design of the machine doesn’t allow access to the burr grinder. The best you can do is jam the end of a spoon or hanger into the receptacle where the beans go into the grinder or turn the entire machine upside down and shake vigorously. Even after doing this, there will likely be residual buildup of coffee grinds in the grinder that can never be accessed or cleaned. This is likely due to the location of the grinder, which is probably less than an inch from the basket. So every time you make a pot of coffee, steam goes straight up into the grinder and coats all the parts with water that the coffee grinds then stick to. After 6 months, I finally called Cuisinart, who told me that they would send me a shipping label so I could send the coffee machine back to them for assessment. I never received anything. Today, after a nice cup of watered down coffee, I performed my weekly routine of cleaning the grinder (ie, turning the machine upside down and shaking), and now the machine just beeps at me and says the hopper is empty, even though it’s completely full. So now I can’t have any coffee unless I grind it myself, which defeats the purpose of this awful machine. I’m ordering a new machine and throwing this one out.Before you write off this 1-Star review as “one of those,“ let me say that when this coffeemaker works, it is awesome. After initial setup, you simply have to make sure there are enough beans in the hopper, add water, insert a clean coffee filter and turn it on. But if any of those steps are inadvertently skipped, at the scheduled brewing time the machine beeps and an alert is displayed. Per the manufacturer’s instructions, when this happens simply complete the skipped step and press the brew button to begin brewing. But once the machine has decided something is amiss, NOTHING will convince it otherwise. Prior to purchasing, I read many reviews that stated the above. But I assumed they were written by malcontents who simply did not understand how to use the machine they had purchased. Let me be a cautionary tale. Sure, there are many glowing reviews and I’m envious of their experiences. But I am writing this with no caffeine in my system because I forgot to add water last night. And once again, after adding water and pressing the manual button, the catch-all “EMPTY” light continues to taunt me. Previously I’ve spoken with tech support who, after a very long hold time, advised me to unplug for a half hour to clear the machine’s memory then reset everything. Did that. They also offered to send a new machine if I continue to have the problem. Support doesn’t open for two more hours, I wanted coffee a half hour ago, who knows how long it will take for a new machine to arrive or what hoops I’ll have to jump through and if I’ll have to box up my current machine and send it back. Plus I’d have to wait on hold again — can’t deal. I just wanted a reliable cup of coffee in the morning and was willing to pay $180 for it. D’oh.On my THIRD one of these now. #2 i've had less than 2 weeks and it's neither grinding, nor brewing. Going to send #2 back in #3's packaging (in case anyone was wondering why I keep ordering the same one). Cuisinart, your brand image is going downhill. Thought you were a decent brand but your QC is sub-par. I'd be open to other options for grind and brew coffeemakers but i'm not seeing any with outstanding reviews.MANUFACTURING DESIGN DEFECT The glass and plastic carafe fails to seal after minimal use. This results in a continuous drip of liquid from carafe onto counter, sink, or floor. My estimate is that I waste and must clean up at least 1 cup of coffee out of every 12. When this continuous flow appeared after a few pots, I thought I just wasn’t pouring it correctly. No. I called Cuisinart product support and 20 minutes later I was told a non-defective replacement was ordered, I need not send back the defective carafe, and 2 weeks later a new carafe arrived.
Yet just days later the same result: dribbling coffee. After some time, thought, and anger I decided to put electrical tape to cover the seam between glass and plastic. One pot of use later, dripping, again. Time passes. I want my purchase details from my Amazon order, and found a “call us” link. I got a call just a few minutes later. The agent took my details, offered to call Cuisinart for me and not too long later, I was connected to Anna who informed me the only thing she could offer is to send me another like carafe. There wasn’t any alternate (I’d thought the all steel carafe used with the 850 model) would work but was told no, AND THAT THERE WAS NO ONE ELSE I COULD SPEAK TO, to resolve my issue.
Amazon will now call back to see if my issue is resolved. Attached is an image from page 5 of the Instruction Booklet. See Item “6. GLASS CARAFE WITH HANDS-FREE LID An easy-pour, no-drip, glass carafe with ergonomic handle” and picture of actual glass plastic carafe.
More as known. It does make very good coffee.We were so excited to get this coffee maker. Our friends had one and loved it so we ordered one as well. It was great for about 6 months and then it began to shut itself off and that’s all it’s done since. You can flip a coin on whether or not it will brew the whole pot and stay on, or just brew a random amount of coffee and shut off.

The hot plate almost never stays on. It’s a small miracle when it does. We really thought we were buying up when we got this one, but after reading similar reviews from others on here, we are going back to other brands. This coffee maker was a massive disappointment. Roll the dice if you want, but if I could do it all again I’d never purchase this junk.While I have a similar product that has worked well for over 5 years at another property, this model stopped working after less than a year. The sensor doesn't appear to be picking up that there is water in it to allow the brewing process to start. Tried to submit a request for support on the manufacturer site multiple times, but it wouldn't allow me to submit claim.
